texts.orig: The work develops  numerically-stable  computer  models to  simulate  the performance  of  cocurrent,  and  countercurrent  membranes. Additionally, a previously  unreported  gas-flow  pattern,  a  variance  of  cross flow,  is discussed and  simulated. These  models  assume  solution-diffusion permeation,  and account for gas non-ideality. A stable  method  is presented  for calculating  the permeate  pressure drop through the  hollow bore of the  membrane fiber. This analysis  also  develops  an  energy  balance  and  calculation  procedures  for simulating the temperature change which occurs in a hollow-fiber membrane.

texts.orig: The cross-flow numerical model is then correlated against data obtained from  a  full-scale,  carbon-dioxide  purification  facility. It  was  noticed  that  this facility's  cellulose  acetate  membranes  exhibited  a  marked  increase  in  gas permeability as the CO2 partial pressure increased. This led to an investigation and correlation of the effects of C02-induced  plasticization  of cellulose  acetate on the increase in mixed-gas permeability. It was found that the permeability of hydrocarbons increases much more than that of CO2 as the CO2 partial pressure increases.  This leads to a decrease in the amount of hydrocarbon that can be recovered  from  a  membrane  unit. This  work  concludes  that  enhanced  oil recovery facilities utilizing cellulose acetate membranes operating at CO2 partial pressures  in  excess  of  approximately  150  psia  could  substantially  benefit  by avoiding  CO2  plasticization  effects  either  by  operating  at  lower  CO2  partial pressures,  or  lowering  the  degree of  acetylation in  the cellulose acetate membrane polymer.

texts.orig: Membrane-based gas-separation has evolved into an extremely important industrial  unit operation  since the first commercial  units were  introduced  in the mid 1970's (Strathmann, 1981).  Membranes are currentiy utilized for numerous gas-separation  processes  including: hydrogen  recovery  from process streams, landfill gas purification, air separation, and the separation of carbon dioxide from methane and  natural gas (Beaver  et al., 1987;  Kurz et  al.,  1986; Schell  et al., 1985;  Bollinger  et  al.,  1982). This  recent  increase  in  the  use  of  membrane technology is primarily attributed to the development of asymmetric membranes (Strathmann, 1975) and hollow-fiber technology (Gardner et al., 1977).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Particulariy  in  enhanced-oil-recovery  (EOR)  facilities  that  purify  carbon dioxide  from  well-head  casing  gas,  membrane  separation  is  a  critical  unit operation.  Membranes at EOR facilities separate the bulk of carbon dioxide gas from methane and natural-gas-liquids  (NGL). Sub-optimal  use of a  membrane unit  in  these  facilities  can  result  in  decreased  methane  fuel-gas  availability, reduced  NGL  production,  and  a  decreased  carbon-dioxide  content  in  the permeate  gas  that  is  reinjected  into  the  oil  reservoir. Decreasing  the  carbon dioxide (CO2) content in oil-field  injectant gas causes the miscibllity pressure of the  C02/water/oil mixture  in  the  oil  reservoir  to  increase,  thereby  possibly decreasing  the  amount  of  oil  recovered  from  the  reservior. Furthermore, membrane  operational  changes  at  EOR  facilities  have  a  profound  effect  on downstream compressors, CO2/NGL fractionation columns, amine systems, and demethanization columns.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As yet, no publicly available computational tools or methods exists which can  predict  the  performance  of  cellulose-triacetate  hollow-fiber  membranes  in service at an EOR facility. Predicting the performance of such membrane units is  a  challenging  problem  due  to  C02-induced  plasticization  of  the  membrane polymer,  non-ideal  gas  behavior,  temperature  effects,  high  permeate  pressure drops in the fiber bore, and previously  unreported gas flow patterns within the membrane module.

Predicting the performance of such membrane units is  a  challenging  problem  due  to  C02-induced  plasticization  of  the  membrane polymer,  non-ideal  gas  behavior,  temperature  effects,  high  permeate  pressure drops in the fiber bore, and previously  unreported gas flow patterns within the membrane module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This analysis was undertaken to: (1) develop a membrane computational model  which  does  not  violate  thermodynamic  principles  and  is  capable  of simulating or assessing the performance of gas separation  membrane modules configured  in cocurrent,  countercurrent,  and  industrial  cross flow patterns; and (2) correlate the effects of C02-induced plasticization of cellulose acetate on the permeabilities of the gas mixture being separated at an EOR facility. Fulfillment of  these  two  objectives  ultimately  leads  the  goal  of  a rigorous computational model capable of being a useful tool for engineers in EOR facilities.

Fulfillment of  these  two  objectives  ultimately  leads  the  goal  of  a rigorous computational model capable of being a useful tool for engineers in EOR facilities.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To  accomplish  this  goal,  the  information  available in  prior literature pertaining to  the  development of  a  membrane  computational model  was investigated. A computational  model based upon solution-diffusion  permeation of a  non-ideal  gas was developed  based  upon thermodynamic  principles. The equations  and  algorithms  describing  temperature  differential,  and  gas  flow patterns through  the  membrane  module  were  also  developed. These  models were  then  compared  to  data  obtained  from  air-  and hydrogen-purification systems, to establish the validity of the developed  models.  The effects of CO2induced plasticization on gas permeability through cellulose-acetate membranes was  then  investigated,  and  a  correlation  was  developed  for  most  of  the  gas components being processed by a typical EOR facility.

texts.orig: Mass transfer through a semi-permeability membrane was first recorded in 1748 by French monks at the Abbe Nollet, who placed wine containers sealed with animal bladders into a vat of water.  No doubt to the consternation of these monks, the wine containers burst due to water osmosis through the bladder (Abbe Nollet, 1748).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since 1748, attempts to understand permeation phenomena has produced a rich history of many theories and almost as many disagreements. This chapter will review the major theories which relate to the modeling of hollow-fiber membranes.  Since several theories are in disagreement, the evidence supporting each theory is discussed, and the theory with the largest body of supporting evidence is utilized In the model development.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 The Debate Over the Way Permeation Occurs
texts.text: 2.2 The Debate Over the Way Permeation Occurs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Scientists and engineers have developed two distinct theories to explain the phenomena of membrane transport.  The  first  theory, called the pore-flow model, was extremely popular up to the mid-1940's, and it is intuitively close to filtration phenomena.  This model assumes that a permeate stream is separated from the feed stream by pressure-driven convective flow through tiny pores. Basically, the pore-flow model assumes that a separation is achieved based upon molecular size, and permeation rates are a function of polymer pore size.
texts.text: Scientists and engineers have developed two distinct theories to explain the phenomena of membrane transport.  The  first  theory, called the pore-flow model, was extremely popular up to the mid-1940's, and it is intuitively close to filtration phenomena.  This model assumes that a permeate stream is separated from the feed stream by pressure-driven convective flow through tiny pores. Basically, the pore-flow model assumes that a separation is achieved based upon molecular size, and permeation rates are a function of polymer pore size.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  second  theory,  called  the solution diffusion  model, assumes  that permeate molecules dissolve in the membrane material and then diffuse across the membrane down a concentration gradient. Therefore, this model assumes that transport  in a  membrane material  is a function of how fast a molecule can diffuse across a membrane {diffusivity, and the solubility oHhe molecule in the membrane material.
texts.text: The  second  theory,  called  the solution diffusion  model, assumes  that permeate molecules dissolve in the membrane material and then diffuse across the membrane down a concentration gradient. Therefore, this model assumes that transport  in a  membrane material  is a function of how fast a molecule can diffuse across a membrane {diffusivity, and the solubility oHhe molecule in the membrane material.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Both  the  pore-flow  and  the  solution-diffusion  theory  appear  to  have scientific  merit,  and  the  debate  as  to  which  model  is the  closest  to  reality  in reverse osmosis membranes was still active as late as 1980.  However, several studies found indications which shifted the body of evidence toward the solution diffusion model (Baker et al., 1972; Thau et al., 1966).  These studies found that the solution diffusion model explained the behavior of reverse osmosis membranes more successfully than the pore-flow model.

These studies found that the solution diffusion model explained the behavior of reverse osmosis membranes more successfully than the pore-flow model.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As gas separation by membranes emerged as a competitive technology In the late 1970's and was applied to CO2 purification in the early 1980's (Bollinger et  al.,  1982;  Mazur  et  al.,  1982),  previous  success  with  the  solution  diffusion model for prediction of reverse osmosis prompted attempts to apply the solution diffusion  model to gas separation. It was found that for  most glassy  polymers such  as  cellulose  acetate  and  polysulfone,  the  solution-diffusion  model  was  a good predictor of membrane performance (Pan, 1983, 1986).

It was found that for  most glassy  polymers such  as  cellulose  acetate  and  polysulfone,  the  solution-diffusion  model  was  a good predictor of membrane performance (Pan, 1983, 1986).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the mid 1980's the discovery of the very unusual glassy polymer poly[1(trimethylsilyl)-l-propyne] (PTMSP)  revived  the  possibility  that  the  pore-flow model could be valid for glassy polymers.  Due to its bulky pendant groups, the permeability of PTMSP is approximately three orders of magnitude greater than that common  membrane  polymers  such  as polysulfone (Ichiraku, 1987). Furthermore,  it has been observed that the permeation  behavior  of PTMSP as temperature  and  pressure  varies  does  not follow  the  trends  predicted  by  the solution diffusion model (Srinivasan, 1994).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recentiy,  it has been suggested that the pore-flow and  solution-diffusion models are both valid for describing  membrane transport, but in membranes of vastiy different pore size (Wijmans et al., 1995).  Wijmans et al. (1995) proposes that  there  exists  a  transition  region  in  membrane  pore  size  above  which  the pore-flow model is valid, and below which the solution-diffusion  model is valid. Polymeric  membranes  with  very  small  pore  sizes,  such  as  polysulfone  or cellulose acetate, follow the solution-diffusion model; whereas, membranes with extremely large pore sizes possibly follow the pore-flow model.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While  the  behavior  of  PTMSP  is  certainly  interesting,  it  is  an  oddity compared  to  the  materials  currentiy  being  used  in  industry. For  common polymers  such  as  cellulose  acetate  or  polysulfone,  the  majority  of  evidence favors  the  solution  diffusion  model;  therefore,  the  analysis  presented  in  this analysis will assume the validity of solution diffusion membrane transport.

texts.orig: Although  broad  commercial  application  of  membrane  technology  has occurred primarily in  the past  20  years,  the  separation of  a mixture  by membranes has been an established idea for well over a century.  The very first membrane calculations and experiments to find concentration profiles, effect of flow direction,  and stage cuts were carried out almost 50 years ago  in several now-classic studies (Weller etal.,  1950; Kammermeyeretal.,  1954, 1955, 1957; Stern etal.,  1965, 1969).

texts.orig: Since  1970,  the  development  of  the  computer  has  made  possible  the evolution of membrane numerical models which vary in rigor from simple linear approximations  to  complex  models  involving  the  simultaneous solution of multiple differential equations.  The authors of the simple linear or the algebraic membrane models generally contend that little is known about membrane fiber dimensions  in a  commercial  unit due to the fact that  most membrane vendors consider this information proprietary.  Therefore, for design purposes, when the dimensions  of the  membrane  units are  unknown,  it is suggested  that complex models cannot claim any more accuracy than the simpler models.  Studies using simple linear or algebraic models have claimed reasonable accuracy over a wide range  of  cut  fractions  and  possible  operating  conditions  (Muthusubramanlan, 1995; Kowali et al., 1994; Rautenbach et al., 1986).

The error is particulariy profound with a significant pressure drop through the fiber bore, or when the  permeate  pressure  is varied  substancially. Thus,  it  is  this  author's opinion that a more  rigorous  approach is necessary.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4 Question of Whether a Pressure or an Activity Difference Causes Permeation
texts.text: 2.4 Question of Whether a Pressure or an Activity Difference Causes Permeation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mass  diffusion  occurs  along  a  concentration  gradient. Increasing  the pressure of a system containing gas increases the number of gas molecules in a given area and thus the concentration of the gas.  Thus, diffusion occurs through a membrane when a pressure differential causes a concentration gradient.  Real gases depart from ideal-gas behavior as increases in pressure do not create as large a  change  in gas concentration,  or density,  as  predicted  by the  ideal-gas law.  The thermodynamic measure of gas concentration is termed activity, which is also equal to the chemical potential of the gas.
texts.text: Mass  diffusion  occurs  along  a  concentration  gradient. Increasing  the pressure of a system containing gas increases the number of gas molecules in a given area and thus the concentration of the gas.  Thus, diffusion occurs through a membrane when a pressure differential causes a concentration gradient.  Real gases depart from ideal-gas behavior as increases in pressure do not create as large a  change  in gas concentration,  or density,  as  predicted  by the  ideal-gas law.  The thermodynamic measure of gas concentration is termed activity, which is also equal to the chemical potential of the gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Almost all the  authors  mentioned  thus far  in this  review  assumed  ideal gas  law  when  calculating  the  concentration  of  the  feed  and  permeate  gas. Certainly, those authors that were analyzing low-pressure systems were justified in  making  that  assumption; however,  very  few  industrial  facilities  which  utilize membranes  operate  at  at  pressures  where  the  assumption  of  gas  ideality  is valid.  Analyses which attempt to model gases in a non-ideal state need to use gas activity, instead of pressure, in the calculation of gas concentration.  To this author's knowledge, only one previous numerical model has utilized the concept that gas activity, not pressure, is the driving force for gas permeation (Fattah et al.,  1992).

texts.orig: Asymmetric  hollow-fiber  membranes  are  manufactured  with  a  dense selective  layer,  supported  by  a  spongy  layer  of  polymer  that  presents  little resistance  to  gas  flow  (see  Figure  2.1). Several  previous  analyses  have assumed that the pressure drop of the gas moving through the porous substrate beneath  the  selective  layer  prevents  the  bulk-permeate  gas  in the fiber bore from mixing with the local permeate gas at the selective layer (Pan, 1983, 1986; Chern,  1985). These  studies  assumed  that  the  local  permeate  mole-fraction should be used to calculate permeate activity, rather than the bulk composition of  the  permeate  gas  in  the  fiber  bore;  however,  these  studies  presented  no evidence to verify this assumption.  Furthermore, these analyses predicted lower permeate flowrates when  correlated  against  actual  data,  particularly  when  the membrane was operated at a high cut fraction (Pan, 1986).

texts.orig: Modeling  the  phenomena  that  occur  in  a  large-scale high-pressure membrane unit, such as what exists in today's enhanced oil recovery (EOR) and hydrogen recovery facilities, requires a model of considerable complexity.  Gas at  high  pressure  exhibits  non-ideal  gas  behavior,  particulariy  when  carbon dioxide compositions are fairiy high.  Furthermore, this non-ideal gas behavior is temperature,  pressure,  and  composition  dependent. An  EOR  facility  utilizing membranes to purify  high-C02 casing  head gas, will  notice that the  permeate and reject gas exits the membrane module as much as 30°F cooler than the feed gas. To  this  author's  knowledge  no  previous  analysis  that  included  any temperature  effects  has  been  reported  in  literature,  even  though  temperature does effect gas activity, polymer permeability, and gas viscosity.

texts.orig: The  membranes  being  operated  in  industry  usually  do  not follow  a  cocurrent or counter-current flow pattern. In one flow scheme, feed gas is evenly distributed along the outside of a fiber bundle, and  flows  through the fiber bundle to a collection  tube at  the center of  the  module. Permeate  gas  flows perpendiculariy to the feed gas and is recovered on both sides of a membrane module. As such, the  separation  characteristics  of these  particular  industrialsize membrane modules are significantiy different than co-current and countercurrent  membrane  modules. The actual gas flow-patterns in industrialsize modules  is  a  subject  that  to  this  author's  knowledge has  not  been addressed in literature, although at least  one  membrane manufacturer's proprietary model does account for this.

texts.orig: Carbon  dioxide  at  high  pressures  solvates  polymers  slightly,  an  effect called plasticization.  Plasticization can increase cellulose acetate's permeability to certain gases by as much as 1000%.  Thus, if plasticization is not accounted for with high pressure gas containing carbon dioxide, a model would be unable to accurately predict permeate flowrates and compositions over a wide range of operating pressures.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The performance of a hollow-fiber membrane in gas service is dependent upon several phenomena:  Gas permeation occurs due to an activity difference between the feed gas and the permeate gas contained  in the fiber bore. The gas permeation rate and permeate composition is strongly dependent  upon the feed  and  permeate  gas flow pattern. As the  permeate  gas flows through the fiber bore  a  pressure  drop  occurs. This  pressure  drop  causes  the  permeate pressure  within  the  fiber  length  to  be  significantiy  higher  than  the  permeate pressure observed on a gauge at the exit of the module. Typically  membrane reject  and  permeate  gas  exits  the  module  at  significantly  lower  temperatures than the feed  gas. This effect  is particularly  pronounced  in those  membranes that  are  purifying  carbon  dioxide  from  well-head  casing  gas. This  chapter discusses the equations  neccessary to model this phenomena  associated with membrane operation.

texts.orig: Enhanced oil-recovery facilities purifying high-pressure C02 from hydrocarbons  will  notice  that  the  gas  exiting  a  membrane  unit  is  significantly cooler than the gas feeding the unit.  The amount of cooling that occurs appears to be a function of the pressure drop between inlet and permeate, composition, and gas cut-fraction. This analysis will assume that there are two phenomena affecting  the  energy  balance  within  a  hollow-fiber  membrane  module:  (1)  the feed gas that permeates undergoes Joule-Thompson effects due to a significant change  in  pressure,  and  (2)  conductive  energy  transfer  occurs  through  the membrane fiber due to differences in inlet and permeate gas temperature.^

texts.orig: calculation of temperature exiting the differential element requires the simultaneous  solution  of  the  BWR  equation  describing  pressure  at  a  known density and temperature, and the BWR equation describing enthalpy at a known density and temperature. Newton's method is a good choice for solving this set of simultaneous equations. Next, the objective function  is evaluated  as simply the calculated temperature difference between exiting feed and permeate gas.  If the  objective  function  is  less  than  the  convergence  criteria  (10"^)  then  heat exchange  due  to  conduction  has  been  found,  and  the  algorithm  is complete. texts.orig: In the  cocurrent flow pattern, the  inlet  gas  is first assumed  to  disperse evenly  in  a  plane  spanning  the  diameter  of  the  fiber  bundle. The  gas  then makes a right angle turn and flows along the length of the membrane fiber. The gas  that  permeates  flows  in  the  same  direction  as  the  feed  gas,  and  exits through a seal, typically made of an expoxy resin, at the end of the module.  The gas  that  does  not  permeate  reaches  the  end  of  the  membrane  and  makes another  right-angle  turn to exit the module.

texts.orig: Dynamic  successive  relaxation  (DSR)  is  much  like  normal  successive relaxation  with  the  exception  that  the  SRF  is varied  based  upon the  previous success  in converging  toward  a  mean  value. If  the  program  detects  that  the previous three  iterations  oscillated, then the  SRF factor  is decreased  in value, and the next guess is approximately the mean of the oscillating values.  On the other  hand,  if the  program detects that the  previous three  interations  were  all moving  in the  same direction, the value  of the  SRF  is increased  and the  next guess  value is  extrapolated based  on  Taylor-series projections from  the information created by previous iterations. Utilizing this concept, the numerical method  of  successive  relaxation  can  be  increased  in  speed  by  an  order  of magnitude  for  certain  equation sets,  and  successive relaxation's inherent problem of calculation instability is eliminated.

texts.orig: The one factor that complicates the simulation of a cocurrent  membrane is the pressure drop through the fiber bore.  At the beginning of the integration, the pressure at the closed end of the fiber bore is not known.  As such, shooting methods which guess the initial permeate  pressure at the top of the fiber bore, and  attempt  to  converge  on  the  known  exiting  permeate  pressure  could  be utilized. Unfortunately,  the  shooting  method  has convergence  problems when the  pressure drop through the fiber bore  is extreme enough that the  permeate pressure at the end of the fiber bore approaches the feed  pressure. This can occur in membranes with small fiber diameters,  membranes whose fibers have collapsed, or membranes with longer fibers.

texts.orig: In reality the membrane modules operating in a cocurrent or countercurrent  fashion  have  a  fraction  of the  feed  gas  bypass  the  membrane fiber bundle and exit the membrane module without undergoing  mass transfer. Only  those  membrane  modules  which  have  large  length-to-diameter  ratios, exhibit behavior that could be precisely modeled as cocurrent or countercurrent. Membrane modules with large length-to-diameter  ratios will have relatively long fibers, and will therefore have an undesirably high fiber-bore pressure drop.

texts.orig: Most of the membranes being utilized  in industry have been engineered to prevent any feed gas from bypassing the membrane fiber bundle. Instead of parallel  gas flow, which  allows  bypassing,  some  industrial  modules  force  the feed  and  permeate  gas to flow perpendicularly  in  relation  to  each  other. To minimize the effect of permeate pressure drop through the fiber, some industrial modules  permeate  on  both  ends  of  the  fiber.  Consequentiy,  the  separation characteristics of industrial modules are substantially different from cocurrent or countercurrent modes of operation.

texts.orig: As can be seen from Figure 3.10, the fiber bundle has a smaller diameter than  the  epoxy  seals  on  each  side  of the  module. This  creates  a  significant amount of clearance  between the fiber bundle and the walls of the  membrane casing.  For this reason,  it is a reasonable assumption that the gas experiences a higher pressure drop moving through the fiber bundle, than distributing around the  outside  of  the  fiber  bundle. Therefore,  it  assumed  that  the  gas  has  a constant  composition  around  the  outside  of the  membrane  bundle,  and flows straight through the fiber bundle toward the reject collection tube.  Due to limited gas movement once the gas enters the fiber bundle, this analysis assumes that the gas experiences no mixing once in the  fiber  bundle.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membranes  are  utilized  in  a  modular  fashion  with  multiple  membrane modules in each stage.  A stage with multiple modules can be simulated much as  previously discussed,  except  the  number  of  fibers  being used  in  the calculation  needs to be multiplied by the number of modules in the stage.  The feed gas flow is assumed to be split evenly between each module; therefore, the whole stage calculation can be lumped into one large module. Stage to stage calculations are done by simply feeding the reject gas flow and composition from the previous stage into each subsequent stage.

The feed gas flow is assumed to be split evenly between each module; therefore, the whole stage calculation can be lumped into one large module. Stage to stage calculations are done by simply feeding the reject gas flow and composition from the previous stage into each subsequent stage.

texts.orig: Carbon  Dioxide  (CO2)  is an  unusual  substance. CO2 above  its critical pressure  and temperature  is known to  be  an  excellent  solvent. This  unusual behavior explains much of the reason of why CO2 was chosen as the injectant gas for  enhanced  oil  recovery  (EOR). Simply  stated,  at  increasing  pressures CO2 is more capable  of solvating  hydrocarbons. This  results  in a  reduction in the viscosity of the oil/water mixture in a reservoir, thereby allowing this mixture to flow more readily to the producing wells.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: higher  pressures  have  been  found  to  permeate  much  more  than  would  be anticipated from observing the performance of the same membranes at the lower pressure facilities. Furthermore, the selectivity of cellulose acetate operating at high  CO2  concentrations  appears  to  be  substantially  less  than  in  facilities operating  at  lower  CO2 concentrations. The  change  in cellulose  acetate gastransport characteristics due  to  CO2  concentration is primarily due  to  a phenomenon called CO2 plasticization, and is the subject of this analysis.

texts.orig: A feature of cellulose is the strong intramolecular hydrogen bonding between the pendant  hydroxyl  groups. This  has the  effect  of  drawing  the  polymer  chains close  to  one  another,  thus  increasing  the  packing  density  and  the  tortional rigidity of  the  material. For  this  reason,  cellulose  is  a  relatively  stiff  and impermeable material.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Shown in Figure 5.2  is a cellulose acetate mer unit with three degrees of acetylation (DA), meaning  that  all the three hydroxyl  groups  have  been substituted with an acetyl group. For the entire  polymer chain, it is possible to have  an  average  degree  of acylation from zero to three. A  highly  substituted cellulose  acetate,  referred  to as cellulose  triacetate  (OTA),  is one  of the  most common polymers in use in the membrane  industry. The addition of the bulky acetyl  groups  decreases  the  packing  density,  and  therefore  increases  the permeability of the polymer.

CO2 at  increasing  pressure  can then disrupt this weak  hydrogen  bonding, an effect referred  to as  plasticization. Simply stated, at  high  partial  pressures,  CO2 behaves  similariy to a solvent  by softening  and slightly  swelling  the  membrane  polymer. texts.orig: The  effects  of  CO2  plasticization  on  the  permeability  of  a  membrane composed of cellulose acetate can be extreme. However,  limited information is available in previous literature on how CO2 plasticization effects the permeability of cellulose  acetate to CO2, nitrogen, and hydrocarbons. Therefore, this study will  attempt to correlate the effects  of CO2 plasticization  on cellulose  acetate's permeability  to  the  common  gas  components  found  in  enhanced  oil  recovery facilities.

texts.orig: At  least  three  previous  studies  have  been  done  regarding  the  effect  of CO2 plasticization on the permeability of gases in cellulose acetate with varying degrees of acetylation. Of note is a study by Stern et al. (1982). Stern found that CO2 solubility in cellulose acetate increases substantially with CO2 pressure and has the effect of "conditioning" the polymer such that methane solubility also increases.  Since permeability is a product of solubility and diffusivity. this study was the first to  suggest  that  the  permeability  of  other  gases  mixed  with  CO2 would increase with increasing CO2 partial pressure.

texts.orig: Again  of  note  is the  study  by  Puleo  et  al.  (1989)  which  found  that  the effects of CO2 plasticization on CO2 permeability in cellulose acetate is not only a function of CO2 partial pressure, but also a function of time and the degree of acetylation of the cellulose acetate.  Puleo found that the permeability of CO2 at pressures  in  excess  of  10  atmospheres  increases  with  time,  and  appears  to reach  a  steady  state  value within the first 200  hours of exposure. This study found  that  cellulose  acetate's  permeability  to  low  pressure  CO2  increases lineariy with an increasing degree of acetylation; however,  Puleo found that the cellulose acetate films with a higher degree of acetylation increased much more in permeability when exposed to high pressure CO2 than the films with a lower degree  of  acetylation. Therefore,  it would  seem that  the  relative  increase  in permeability of cellulose acetate due to CO2 plasticization is a strong function of degree of acetylation.

texts.orig: To answer the question of how CO2 plasticization effects the permeabilities of the gas mixture being separated at a typical EOR facility, data was  obtained  for  this  study  from  the  Wiser  Wellman  facility  located  near Brownfield, Texas. This facility  uses hollow-fiber  cellulose  acetate  membranes to  purify  CO2 from methane  and  NGL. The typical  operating  conditions  of the Wiser  Wellman  plant  during  the  time  period  of  this  study  was  a  feed  gas composition  of  85%  CO2  being  fed  to  a five-stage membrane  unit  operating between  350  and  450  psia  feed  pressure  and  70°F  feed  temperature. Gas composition  was  analyzed  by  gas  chromatography  (GO)  on  each  stage  inlet, permeate, and reject gas.  Flowrates of the inlet to the main membrane train, and the first stage permeate gas were obtained, as well as operating pressures and temperatures of each stage's inlet and permeate gas.

texts.orig: Figure 5.3  shows the calculated  relative  permeability  index (RPI)  of CO2 versus the average CO2 partial pressure of the stage.  The results of Puleo et al. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Calculating the permeability of each of these gases at a high CO2 partial pressure  requires  the  use  of  the  gases'  permeability  at  a  low  CO2  partial pressure. It is impossible to report absolute permeabilities, since the selectivelayer  thickness  of  these  membranes  is  unknown. However,  it  is  possible  to report  the  permeabilities  of each  of these  gases  relative  to  CO2. This  value calculated  as  the  permeability  of  CO2  divided  by  the  permeability  of  that particular gas is usually referred to as the alpha selection factor.

texts.orig: 5.5.2 Calculating Permeability of a Gas at a Particular CO2 Partial Pressure
texts.text: 5.5.2 Calculating Permeability of a Gas at a Particular CO2 Partial Pressure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeability of the gases considered in this study can be calculated at any particular CO2 partial pressure, by using the RPI correlations developed eariier, the alpha selection factors presented in Table 5.2, and a known value of CO2 permeability.  Again, this study can not report an absolute CO2 permeability, since  the  thickness  of the  membrane's  selective  layer  is  not  known  precisely. What  can  be  reported  is  the permeance, a  value  which  is  defined  as  the permeability divided by the selective  layer thickness. After  accounting for CO2 plasticization, the average CO2 permeance of these cellulose acetate membranes in a non-plasticized state, was found to be approximately 2.0 x 10^ cm^(STP)/(cm^ cmHg sec).

texts.orig: 5.6 The Effect of CO, Plasticization on Cellulose Acetate's Selectivity
texts.text: 5.6 The Effect of CO, Plasticization on Cellulose Acetate's Selectivity

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At  first  glance, CO2  plasticization could be considered a positive phenomenon, since it increases permeability, thereby decreasing the number of membranes  needed  for  a  particular  gas  service. However,  CO2 plasticization has a negative impact on  membrane  selectivity, which  causes a larger proportion  of  an  EOR  facility's  possible  fuel  gas  and  NGL  to  escape  in  the permeate.
texts.text: At  first  glance, CO2  plasticization could be considered a positive phenomenon, since it increases permeability, thereby decreasing the number of membranes  needed  for  a  particular  gas  service. However,  CO2 plasticization has a negative impact on  membrane  selectivity, which  causes a larger proportion  of  an  EOR  facility's  possible  fuel  gas  and  NGL  to  escape  in  the permeate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membranes at a typical enhanced oil-recovery  (EOR) facility  are  usually economically  justified  by  providing  fuel-gas  and  natural  gas  liquids  (NGL)  at much  lower  operating  costs  compared  to  conventional distillation. When comparing the economics of membranes and distillation systems, most analyses look  primarily  at  operating  costs;  however,  a  complete  analysis  should  also consider  the  monetary  value  of the  hydrocarbon  losses  in the  permeate  of a membrane  plant. A  conventional  lean-oil  distillation  plant,  such  as  the  RyanHolmes process, will capture in excess of 90% of the inlet hydrocarbon for sale as fuel  gas  and  NGL  product. In  contrast,  this  author  has  experience  with  a high-pressure  membrane facility which operates  at  an  average first-stage CO2 partial pressure of 450 psia.  This particular facility loses as much as 50% of the methane  in the  membrane  feed  gas to the  permeate. texts.orig: Cellulose acetate membranes installed at an EOR facility exhibit relatively unusual behavior when compared to the predictable membranes installed at hydrogen- and air-separation facilities.  As discussed previously, the unusual membrane behavior at EOR facilities is attributable to non-ideal gas effects, CO2 induced plasticization, and mixed-phase operation.  Several of these peculiar phenomena that in this author's experience have created problems, or puzzlement, at an EOR facility are simulated and briefly discussed in this chapter.

texts.orig: A BRIEF CASE STUDY ON THE EFFECT OF CO2 PLASTICIZATION ON EOR FACILITY DESIGN AND ECONOMICS
texts.text: A BRIEF CASE STUDY ON THE EFFECT OF CO2 PLASTICIZATION ON EOR FACILITY DESIGN AND ECONOMICS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Previous  analysis  (Chapter  V)  suggests  that  due  to  plasticization,  the selectivity  of  cellulose  triacetate  to  permeate  CO2  over  hydrocarbons  falls  by approximately  a  factor  of  three  to five as  the  CO2  partial  pressure  increases beyond  150  psia. As  such,  EOR  facilities  operating  at  high  CO2  partial pressures  can  not make  as clean  a separation,  and  inevitably  permeate  large amounts of hydrocarbon.  This additional hydrocarbon loss is partially offset by a decrease in the number of membranes necessary for the separation, due to the increase in permeability associated with plasticization.  The question is whether it is more cost effective to utilize membranes in a this plasticized state, or design the  facility  to  avoid  CO2  plasticization  effects. The  following  case  study  will briefly  compare  the  economics  of  a  facility  operating at  high  CO2  partial pressures,  to two  hypothetical  facilities  designed  to  avoid  the  phenomenon  of CO2 plasticization.
texts.text: Previous  analysis  (Chapter  V)  suggests  that  due  to  plasticization,  the selectivity  of  cellulose  triacetate  to  permeate  CO2  over  hydrocarbons  falls  by approximately  a  factor  of  three  to five as  the  CO2  partial  pressure  increases beyond  150  psia. As  such,  EOR  facilities  operating  at  high  CO2  partial pressures  can  not make  as clean  a separation,  and  inevitably  permeate  large amounts of hydrocarbon.  This additional hydrocarbon loss is partially offset by a decrease in the number of membranes necessary for the separation, due to the increase in permeability associated with plasticization.  The question is whether it is more cost effective to utilize membranes in a this plasticized state, or design the  facility  to  avoid  CO2  plasticization  effects. The  following  case  study  will briefly  compare  the  economics  of  a  facility  operating at  high  CO2  partial pressures,  to two  hypothetical  facilities  designed  to  avoid  the  phenomenon  of CO2 plasticization.

texts.orig: It becomes more difficult to take advantage of the increased selectivity of these membranes at low CO2 partial pressures as the CO2 content in the plant's feed gas is increased.  For example, a facility with 85% CO2 in its feed gas could compress  the  feed  gas  up  to  only  180  psia  before  feeding  it  into  the first membrane stage. Many more membrane modules would be necessary, as the possible  pressure  differential  is fairiy  small. Therefore,  the  economics  of this type of design would probably  not be favorable for a feed gas containing 85% CO2. Conversely,  a facility  being fed a gas stream containing  50% CO2 could compress  the  feed  gas  to  300  psia  before  beginning  membrane  separation. Fewer  modules  would  be  necessary,  as  the  possible  pressure  differential  is greater.  Furthermore, this facility with an inlet gas stream of 50% CO2 has much more methane and ethane to lose compared to a facility with 75% CO2 in the feed gas. Therefore, facilities with feed gas compositions containing approximately  75%  002  or  less  should  consider  avoiding  the  effects  of  002 plasticization,  through  design  efforts,  or  changing  the  membrane's  degree  of acetylation, as discussed in the next section.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: plasticization  effects. It  has  been  observed  that  cellulose  acetate  with  a  2.45 degree  of acetylation  (DA)  does  not plasticize  appreciably  up to a  CO2 partial pressure of 450 psia (Puleo et al., 1989).  Thus, a 2.45  DA membrane would be expected to exhibit the typical selectivity of cellulose acetate, even at high CO2 partial  pressures. The  disadvantage  of  this  is  that  lowering  the  degree  of acetylation of a cellulose acetate film from 2.85  to 2.45  was found to lower the CO2 permeability of the film by 28% (Puleo et al., 1989).  This decrease in CO2 permeability, coupled with the remission of CO2 plasticization effects, necessitates the use of a greater number of membrane modules to process the same volume of gas.

texts.orig: '  The selectivity of cellulose acetate membranes with a 2.45 degree of acetylation would be expected to be better than the values reported in Table 5.2; therefore, this is considered a highly conservative estimate.  Additionally, several of the RPI correlations showed a marked decrease in hydrocarbon permeability until the point where plasticization effects began increasing the membrane's permeability.  It stands to reason that were the trend for a decrease in hydrocarbon permeability to continue unaffected by CO2 plasticization, the alpha selection factors utilized in this portion of the analysis could be significantly less than what might be observed.  If this is the case, this analysis will underestimate permeate CO2 purities, and hydrocarbon recoveries.

texts.orig: It should be stated that higher rates of return could probably be achieved by optimizing the design of these facilities.  Design optimization could be done by utilizing multiple stages to decrease compression requirements; however, the increased cost of membrane surface area has to be balanced against decreased compression costs.  The increased permeation rate due to CO2 plasticization has to be balanced against the relative worth of the hydrocarbon being lost in the permeate.  Furthermore, facilities could possibly utilize membranes with a lower degree of acetylation in the initial stages where the CO2 partial pressure is above 150 psia, and then use membranes with a higher degree of acetylation in the later stages.  Thus,  finding  a truly optimal design is an extremely complex problem that could probably only be properiy solved using linear programming techniques.

texts.orig: It is evident that CO2 plasticization increases the permeability of cellulose acetate membranes; thereby, decreasing the number of membrane modules and thus the capital investment required to process a given volume of gas.  However, the decrease in membrane selectivity associated with CO2 plasticization is highly detrimental to the possible cash flow of an EOR facility.  Cleariy, it is in the best economic interest of these facilities to avoid membrane plasticization.
